coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots supported zinc oxide (ZnO/N-CQD) nanoflower photocatalyst for methylene blue photodegradation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Widiyandari, Hendri
Prilita, Orien
Al Ja'farawy, Muhammad Shalahuddin
Nurosyid, Fahru
Arutanti, Osi
Astuti, Yayuk
Mufti, Nandang
coredata.dc:description: This research highlights the three-step hydrothermal synthesis of N-CQD and its effect on the physical properties and photocatalytic activity of ZnO/N-CQD compared to ZnO commercial and the prepared ZnO nanoflower. The physical and optical properties of photocatalysts were conducted to investigate the correlation with photodegradation of Methylene Blue (MB) under visible light. During 30 min, MB concentration decreased to 80% by the prepared ZnO/N-CQD. This result showed superior activity compared to ZnO commercial and ZnO nanoflowers. Considering the high photocatalytic performance of ZnO/N-CQD, it can be further used as an efficient photocatalyst for organic pollutant removal from aqueous.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
ZnO nanoflower
Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal
Photodegradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CQD, and digital photograph of N-CQD solution without (left) and with (right) UV light irradiation (inset), (b) Zeta potential of N-CQD and ZnO/N-CQD.
(a) The XRD pattern, (b) The shifted diffraction peaks, and (c) FTIR spectra of ZnO-type photocatalyst.
SEM images of (a) ZnO; (b) ZnO Nanoflower; (c) ZnO/N-CQD, and (d) HRTEM image of ZnO/N-CQD.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and (b) dV/dD pore volume vs pore diameter of ZnO, ZnO nanoflower, and ZnO/N-CQD.
The optical properties of the prepared photocatalyst: (a) Diffuse reflectance UV–Vis spectra, (b) band gap energy, (c–d) PL spectra.

Photocatalytic activity (a), and the pseudo-first-order kinetic of Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L–H) curves (b) of the ZnO, ZnO nanoflower, ZnO/N-CQD, controls for the photodegradation of MB under simulated sunlight irradiation, (c) photograph of decoloration of MB using ZnO/N-CQD, and (d) Photocatalytic reusability of the ZnO/N-CQD for the degradation of MB with three cycling runs under simulated sunlight irradiation.
XRD data of ZnO, ZnO nanoflower, ZnO/N-CQD preferred (101) crystal plane.
BET analysis surface area and porosity of ZnO, ZnO nanoflower, ZnO/N-CQD.
Kinetic equation and photodecomposition rate of MB solution in a different photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots supported zinc oxide (ZnO/N-CQD) nanoflower photocatalyst for methylene blue photodegradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research highlights the three-step hydrothermal synthesis of N-CQD and its effect on the physical properties and photocatalytic activity of ZnO/N-CQD compared to ZnO commercial and the prepared ZnO nanoflower. The physical and optical properties of photocatalysts were conducted to investigate the correlation with photodegradation of Methylene Blue (MB) under visible light. During 30 min, MB concentration decreased to 80% by the prepared ZnO/N-CQD. This result showed superior activity compared to ZnO commercial and ZnO nanoflowers. Considering the high photocatalytic performance of ZnO/N-CQD, it can be further used as an efficient photocatalyst for organic pollutant removal from aqueous.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methylene blue (3,7-bis(dimethylamino) phenothiazine chloride tetra methylthionine chloride) dye is a popular cationic dye that is environmentally persistent, carcinogenic, toxic, and mutagenic [1]. It is commonly used as a synthetic dye in the clothing and textile industries, as well as in the dyeing of papers and leathers. A large volume of methylene blue dye-containing wastewater is discharged into groundwater and surface water as a result of the magnitude of industrial usage. The monoamine oxidate inhibitory properties of methylene blue (MB) dye can cause fatal serotonin toxicity in humans at doses greater than 5 mk/kg, in addition to posing a threat to aquatic fauna. As a result, removing MB dye from wastewaters is critical.

Various removal strategies for MB dye wastewater have been reported in the literature. The various sub-separation methods proposed for removing MB from effluent and sewage are chemical/electrocoagulation, oxidation, biodegradation, biocatalytic degradation, adsorption, and photocatalyzed degradation. Among these methods, photocatalysis technology using metal-oxide semiconductor material is one of the most promising options. The advantages of photocatalysis technology are low price because the raw material is abundant and cheap; environmentally friendly because of converting organic contaminants to CO2 and H2O; energy saving because of working under ambient conditions; and sustainable technology because photocatalyst has excellent catalyst stability. Recently, more solar photocatalysts have been developed to improve the environment by utilizing solar energy. Among various metal oxides, ZnO nanoparticles have become the center of attention due to their advantages, such as cheaper, non-corrosive, and large binding excitation energy [2]. The structure of ZnO nanoparticles can change easily into shapes such as nanowire [3], nanorods [4,5], nanoneedles [6], and nanoflowers [7]. The ZnO nanoflower structure has the largest surface area. Unfortunately, ZnO's large band gap, the faster electron-hole recombination, the poor photostability during light exposure, and low adsorption capacity are the drawback of ZnO for the photocatalysis application [8,9].

Carbon quantum dots (CQD), a newly discovered quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have piqued the interest of researchers due to their broad absorption band, high optical absorption coefficient, and chemical stability. Furthermore, the CQD's unique up-conversion luminescence can be excited by light of higher wavelengths and lower frequencies, and the CQD can then emit low-wavelength light of higher frequencies. In addition, CQD doped with nonmetallic elements (N, P, S, Cl) can have more stable optical properties, higher quantum yields, and excellent catalytic performances [10,11]. Furthermore, when combined with a photocatalyst semiconductor, CQD can be used as electron acceptors to reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increasing the optical absorption, and thus enhance photocatalytic activity [12].

Accordingly, the research proposed synthesizing ZnO/N-CQD nanoflower materials by a simple and low-cost method. Furthermore, the physicochemical properties of the prepared ZnO/N-CQD nanoflower were evaluated and compared with the ZnO commercial. The result shows that the prepared ZnO/N-CQD nanoflower has interesting photocatalytic properties.

Zinc oxide nanoflower was prepared using an analytical grade of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), trisodium citrate dehydrates (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, Merck, Germany), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Merck, Germany). While the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot was synthesized using urea (CO(NH2)2, Merck, Germany) and citric acid (C6H8O7, PT. Brataco, Indonesia). Ethanol (C2H5OH, Merck, Germany) and distilled water were used for washing the sample. All chemicals were used without any further purification.

In preparation for ZnO nanoflower, 3.72 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 1.47 g C6H5Na3O7·2H2O were added into 10 mL distilled water. The 1 M NaOH solution was added to the mixture drop by drop under stirring until the pH value was 13. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 100 min at room temperature and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for the hydrothermal process at a temperature of 130 °C for 10 h in the furnace. The product was separated using filter paper and washed with H2O and ethanol. After the washing process, the residue was collected and dried at 100 °C for 4 h. The pure ZnO-like flower was fabricated by calcination at 500 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1.

In order to prepare N-CQD, 2 mmol of citric acid and 6 mmol of urea CO(NH2)2 were dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water under stirring for 10 min and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave for the hydrothermal process for 8 h at 160 °C. The photocatalyst ZnO/N-CQD was prepared by dispersed 0.2 g of ZnO-like flower into 45 mL of H2O. Further, 1.5 mL of N-CQD solution was added and continued stirring for 2 h. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 100 °C for 8 h. After washing, the residue was collected and dried at 100 °C for 4 h.

The crystallographic properties of the final products were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Smartlab equipped with Cu Kα radiation: λ = 1.5406 Å) in the 2θ range from 20 to 80°. The Fourier Transmission Infra-Red (FTIR, Shimadzu IR Spirit) was performed to examine the functional group of the final product. The Field Emission-Scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, FESEM JIB-4610F) was applied to observe the surface morphology. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 20S-Twin) was used to study the microstructure of N-CQD. The energy band gap of the samples was measured using the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance (MAYA Pro2000 Ocean Optics). Photoluminescence (PerkinElmer LS 55) was performed to examine the electron-hole recombination. The UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer's Lambda 25) was performed to measure the absorbance of MB solution before and after being treated by photocatalyst and irradiation, and N-CQD. The zeta potential was measured by Horiba SZ-100. The surface area of the samples and N2 adsorption-desorption measurements were performed at 77 K on a Micromeritics Tristar II Plus 3020 instrument to determine the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and porosity properties.

The photocatalytic activity of ZnO/N-CQD nanoflower was tested using photodegradation of 10 ppm Methylene Blue solution under visible light irradiation (solar simulator, Peccel/PEC-L01). The distance of the lamp to the sample was adjusted to 11 cm. Samples were taken at 0.1 g and mixed with 50 mL Methylene Blue solution. The test was repeated for 105 min in every sample, with the first 30 min carried out in the dark and the next 75 min under visible light irradiating. After photodegradation, the samples were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min to separate photocatalyst powder and decolored Methylene Blue solution. The absorbance of decolored Methylene Blue solution was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer, which is the decrease of the absorbance related to the decreasing concentration of samples.

The UV–Vis spectra of N-CQD solution are depicted in Fig. 1 (a). Meanwhile, the inset figure displays a digital image of the N-CQD solution under UV irradiation (right), and without UV irradiation (left). The specific absorption peak at 268 nm associated with the π-π* transition of electrons in the conjugated CC band, as well as an adsorption peak at 300–400 nm associated with the n- π * transition of CN, C–N, and CO bonds [10,13]. Fig. 1(b) shows the zeta potential value of N-CQD and ZnO/N-CQD. During the process, the zeta potential of ZnO/N-CQD (−22 eV) was more negatively charged than N-CQD (-9eV). The result shows the negatively charged surface of N-CQD caused by carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of N-CQD. In addition, ZnO/N-CQD has more negative than N-CQD due to the interaction in the ZnO.

As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the prepared ZnO/N-CQD has characteristics of a hexagonal wurtzite structure with all diffraction peaks corresponding to crystal plane (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201). This pattern is well-matched with JCPDS 36–1451 data of ZnO. The N-CQD species were not observed in the diffraction peaks due to the small amount (<10%), high dispersion, and low crystallinity of N-CQD [7]. The intensities of the characteristic diffraction peaks in ZnO/N-CQD were significantly lower than in pure ZnO. This phenomenon could be caused by some of the X-rays being absorbed by the carbon quantum dots loaded on ZnO. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks for the (100); (002); (101) crystal plane of ZnO/N-CQD are shifted to a greater angle (Fig. 2(b)), which could be attributed to the interaction between ZnO and the decorated carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) [14]. The c/a value was close to a reference of 1.602, which indicated that all samples were in an excellent hexagonal closed packed (hcp) [15]. While. Table 1 shows that the crystallite size of ZnO/N-CQD is smaller than other ZnO. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 2(c)) shows the functional groups to distinguish the presence of nitrogen. The characteristic absorption band at 1558 cm−1 and 1457 cm−1 at N-CQD attributed to the CC and C–N stretching vibrations. The absorption in 1623 cm−1 and 1432 cm−1 of sample ZnO/N-CQD represent CC and C–N stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of N-CQD on the surface of ZnO.

The morphology of the prepared particles was shown in Fig. 3 . From the SEM images (Fig. 3 (a)–(c)), the structure of commercial ZnO, ZnO nanoflower, and ZnO/N-CQD was nanorods, nanorods and nanoflowers mix structured, and complete nanoflowers structure, respectively. The particle size of the prepared particles increased from 200, 419, and 616 nm. The HR-TEM image could detect the presence of N-CQD in Fig. 3(d). Based on the HR-TEM image, nanoflower ZnO/N-CQD was formed by heterogeneous small ZnO primer particles with a size of 50 nm and lattice fringe 0.24 nm. Furthermore, five nm of N-CQD with a lattice fringe of 0.18 nm was well distributed on the surface of ZnO/N-CQD photocatalyst. It can be inferred that this method has successfully prepared photocatalyst ZnO-modified N-CQD on the surface.

Gas adsorption analysis results of the ZnO, ZNO nanoflower, and ZnO/NCQD, as seen in Fig. 4 , all showed adsorption/desorption isotherms belonging to type IV characterized by a hysteresis loop. The hysteresis patterns indicated that the samples are porous materials since they adsorb N2 gas. Based on the data presented in Table 2 , all samples are classified as mesoporous materials which have pore size 2–50 nm. Table 2 also indicates that ZnO/N-CQD has the highest surface area eventhough the pore size is smaller than ZnO nanoflower. It can be concluded that ZnO/N-CQD contains more pores than others. Furthermore, the pore size distribution as shown in Fig. 4(b) indicates that the pore sizes of the three samples are not homogeneous as indicated by several peaks. Fig. 4(b) also marks that the pore volume of ZnO/N-CQD is higher than that of other samples. Therefore, it assumes that ZnO/N-CQD has the highest number of pores or the biggest pore volumes which results in the highest surface area. The higher the surface area the more adsorption to occur.

To reveal the functions of the decorated carbon quantum dots on the ZnO nanoflowers, the UV–Vis DRS and PL spectra of these photocatalysts were comprehensively studied. Using UV–Vis DRS, the band gap energy value of the photocatalysts has similar strong UV and visible absorption characteristic peaks in 320–390 nm and 400–800 nm, respectively (Fig. 5 (a)). The electronic transition n-π* of the CO bond from the core of N-CQD and the transition from π-π* of the CC bond from the surface of N-CQD [16], making the wavelength of ZnO slightly red-shifted it called quantum confinement effect. Consequently, the band gap energy value of ZnO decreased from 3.176 to 3.129 eV (Fig. 5 (b)), and the conduction band rose [17]. PL Spectra measured photogenerated charge separation and recombination behaviors at 395 nm (Fig. 5 (c)–(d)). The peak of ZnO/N-CQD around 485 nm was lower than that of ZnO commercial and nanoflowers. The intense emission peaks at 421 and 447 nm were possibly caused by the interface radiation and defects of ZnO nanoflower [14], while the blue-green light emission at 485 nm was related to intrinsic surface defects in the ZnO nanoflower lattice [18]. The green emission peaks at 527 and 540 nm were attributed to the near band-edge emission from the recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes [16]. Conversely, PL emission intensity is proportional to the higher recombination rate of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Vice versa, the defect sits on the ZnO/N-CQD. Trapping the electrons triggered the rapid charge transfer and decreased the recombination rate [19]. Accordingly, the presence of N-CQD is expected to increase photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic performance of ZnO/N-CQD towards MB under visible light irradiation is shown in Fig. 6 (a), (b). The presence of photocatalyst material succeeds in decomposed MB over time. The result shows that in 30 min, the prepared ZnO has the lowest photocatalytic activity (10% degradation), while the ZnO/N-CQD showed the best photocatalytic performance (80% degradation). The decoloration of MB using ZnO/N-CQD is shown in Fig. 6 (c). Even though the ZnO/N-CQD has the largest particle size, the photodegradation showed superior activity due to the largest surface area, lowest value of band gap and PL intensity. The transfer of electrons and holes was the main factor in improving photocatalytic performance. The narrow bandgap enhanced the light absorption spectra and facilitated electron transfer [20]. Under dark condition, the different of zeta potential did not influence the MB adsorption process. It can be inferred that photodegradation of MB efficiency was mainly due to the optical and physical properties of the prepared photocatalyst as the presence of N-CQD. The presence of N-CQD succeeded in avoiding electron-hole recombination and produced the radical hydroxyl (OH*) continuously. In addition, the work function of N-CQD below the conduction band energy of ZnO created the stronger electronic coupling of π electrons of carbon with the conduction band of ZnO and led to the up-conversion [21]. As a result, the photocatalytic efficiency and the rate of ZnO/N-FCQD showed the highest [21]. The kinetic degradation of MB solution under visible light follows a pseudo-first-order kinetic reaction calculated by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) model, as shown in Fig. 6 (b). Photodecomposition rate was proposed in Table 3 . Furthermore, the reusability of ZnO/N-CQD was demonstrated through three cycles of photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the degradation percentage of MB was 86% in the second cycle and 47% in the third cycle, demonstrating good ZnO/N-CQD reusability. The result was most likely due to the effectively embedded pattern of N-CQD inside the ZnO nanoflowers, which limited N-CQD loss during the reaction [22].

Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dot (N-CQD) modified ZnO has been successfully prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. The presence of N-CQD succeeds in the avoidance of electron-holes recombination. After 30 min, the ZnO/N-CQD decreased the MB to 80%. This result was 30% and 70% higher than that of ZnO commercial and ZnO nanoflower, respectively. Therefore, this research could provide an excellent strategy to increase the photocatalytic activity.

Hendri Widiyandari: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Orien Prilita: Methodology, Visualization, Investigation, Data acquisition. Muhammad Shalahuddin Al Ja'farawy: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Fahru Nurosyid: Writing – review & editing. Osi Arutanti: Methodology, writing-review & editing. Yayuk Astuti: Writing-review & editing. Nandang Mufti: BET measurement & manuscript editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Research and Technology Republic of Indonesia under the grant of Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia with contract number 872.1/UN27.22/PT.01.03/2022.
